disease.name: Gallstone Ileus
disease.note: Rare surgical cause of mechanical small bowel obstruction caused by a gallstone passing through a cholecystenteric fistula (usually cholecystoduodenal) and becoming impacted in the distal ileum. Typically affects elderly females with long-standing gallstone disease. Classic radiographic triad: pneumobilia, small bowel obstruction, and ectopic gallstone (Rigler triad). High morbidity and mortality (15-20%) due to advanced age and delayed diagnosis.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Bowel obstruction -> EMERGENCY surgery; Stable -> URGENT

historyFeatures.note: Tumbling stone phenomenon: stone moves and obstruction temporarily relieves
Uncommon; fistula usually decompresses biliary tree

imaging.name: Abdominal X-ray (erect + supine)
imaging.indication: Rigler triad: pneumobilia, SBO with dilated loops, radio-opaque gallstone (only 10-20% of stones are visible)

imaging.name: CT abdomen with IV contrast
imaging.indication: definitive: SBO with transition point, gallstone at transition, cholecystoduodenal fistula, pneumobilia

management.protocolReference: WSES Guidelines for Gallstone Ileus; SAGES Guidelines

pathways.criteria: Small bowel obstruction + gallstone at transition point + pneumobilia
pathways.treatment: Resuscitation: IV fluids, NG tube decompression, correct electrolytes, antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole). Surgery is definitive: enterolithotomy alone (stone extraction through enterotomy) vs one-stage surgery (enterolithotomy + cholecystectomy + fistula closure). Enterolithotomy alone is preferred for elderly/unstable (lower morbidity and mortality). One-stage reserved for young/fit patients. Stone typically impacted at terminal ileum (narrowest part of small bowel). Milk stone proximally and remove via longitudinal enterotomy. Check for second stone (10% have multiple). Postoperative: monitor for bile leak, sepsis, recurrence.
